communications.subject: Freedom of Information Act Request: Spirit Airlines Communications
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552 et seq., and the regulatory implementation of the Department of the Treasury at 31 C.F.R. Part 1, I am submitting this request for records regarding the Department’s communications, term sheets, internal memoranda, and contingency plans relating to the proposed federal bailout of Spirit Aviation Holdings, Inc. and its affiliates (collectively, "Spirit Airlines") prior to its May 2, 2026 operational liquidation.

For the timeframe of January 1, 2026, through May 2, 2026, we request the production of the following:
I. REQUESTED RECORDS
1)Bailout Term Sheets and Equity Plans: All internal memoranda, draft term sheets, and financial modeling produced or reviewed by the Treasury Department—including the Office of Domestic Finance—detailing the proposed $500 million debtor-in-possession (DIP) loan or bailout package, and the corresponding warrants that would have granted the U.S. government an ownership stake in Spirit Airlines.

2)Creditor Communications: All records of communication (including emails, text messages, encrypted messaging logs, and calendar entries) between any Treasury official and representatives of Spirit Airlines' major creditors—explicitly including but not limited to Citadel, Ares Management Corp., and Cyrus Capital—regarding the proposed federal financing package and their subsequent rejection of the government's terms.

3)Internal Risk Assessments: All Treasury Department briefing materials, risk assessments, and interagency communications (particularly with the Department of Transportation and the Executive Office of the President) analyzing Spirit's liquidity drain, the impact of surging jet fuel prices, and the potential exposure of taxpayer funds to Spirit's Chapter 22 bankruptcy (Case No. 25-11897).

II. PUBLIC ACKNOWLEDGMENT OF DIALOGUE

The Treasury Department cannot withhold these records under blanket claims of executive or deliberative privilege, as the existence of these bailout negotiations has been extensively acknowledged by the Executive Branch and widely reported in the public domain.
In late April 2026, multiple outlets, including The Wall Street Journal, Bloomberg, and CBS News, confirmed that the administration was in "advanced negotiations" to provide $500 million in government financing to Spirit. (See CBS News, "Talks to bail out Spirit Airlines stall as company teeters toward collapse," April 29, 2026. https://www.cbsnews.com/news/talks-to-bail-out-spirit-airlines-stall-as-company-teeters-toward-collapse/).

Furthermore, the President publicly confirmed the administration's willingness to execute a federal takeover of the airline "for the right price," citing the potential preservation of thousands of jobs. Because the Executive Branch has already disclosed the core parameters of the bailout and its dialogue with Spirit and its creditors, the Treasury Department has waived the right to conceal the underlying documents detailing how these taxpayer funds were leveraged in negotiations.

III. FEE WAIVER JUSTIFICATION
We request a complete waiver of all search, review, and duplication fees associated with processing this request. Under 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(4)(A)(iii) and 31 C.F.R. § 1.5(b), an agency must waive fees when "disclosure of the information is in the public interest because it is likely to contribute significantly to public understanding of the operations or activities of the government and is not primarily in the commercial interest of the requester." This request easily clears every legal threshold for a mandatory fee waiver:

1. The Subject Matter Concerns the Operations and Activities of the Federal Government. The requested records strike at the very heart of the Treasury Department's core operations: the management, allocation, and risk assessment of federal funds. The negotiation of a $500 million federal lifeline to a private corporation is inherently a government activity.

2. Disclosure is Meaningfully Informative and Significantly Contributes to Public Understanding. The collapse of Spirit Airlines is the first total operational liquidation of a major U.S. airline in a generation, and the proposed federal intervention was equally historic. Had it succeeded, the government would have executed a shocking quasi-nationalization of an American airline. The public has been left in the dark regarding how the Treasury calculated the viability of such an extreme measure, especially after Spirit's prior restructuring failed. Disclosure will provide unprecedented insight into whether the Treasury was acting to stabilize a vital economic sector or engaging in politically motivated market intervention. Courts have repeatedly recognized that when a request involves high-profile federal financial interventions and the direct exposure of hundreds of millions in taxpayer dollars, the "significant contribution" test is satisfied. See Better Gov't Ass'n v. Dep't of State, 780 F.2d 86 (D.C. Cir. 1986); McClellan Ecological Seepage Situation v. Carlucci, 835 F.2d 1282 (9th Cir. 1987). MuckRock possesses the operational capacity and express intent to disseminate these critical findings to the public at large.


The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

SAM RICHARDSON

communications.subject: Freedom of Information Act Request: Spirit Airlines Communications
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

I. REQUESTED RECORDS
For the timeframe of January 1, 2026, through May 2, 2026, we request the production of the following:
External Communications: All records of communication (including emails, memoranda, text messages, encrypted messaging logs, and calendar entries) between any DOT official—expressly including Secretary Sean Duff and their immediate staff—and any executive, legal representative, or registered lobbyist for Spirit Airlines regarding the airline’s financial solvency, liquidity, debt restructuring, or bankruptcy filings (Case Nos. 24-11988 and 25-11897).
Federal Bailout Deliberations: All internal and external DOT communications concerning the proposed federal bailout and government stake acquisition involving Spirit Airlines, which Secretary Duffy and other officials publicly acknowledged in early May 2026.
Internal Solvency Assessments: All internal DOT memoranda, briefing materials, or risk assessments analyzing Spirit Airlines’ financial viability, the impact of rising oil prices on its ultra-low-cost model, and the contingency planning for consumer refunds following an operational shutdown.

II. PUBLIC ACKNOWLEDGMENT OF DIALOGUE
The existence of these records is indisputable, as DOT leadership has publicly acknowledged deep, ongoing involvement in Spirit’s financial crisis. Following Spirit’s sudden cancellation of all flights on May 2, 2026, Secretary Duffy gave explicit public statements regarding the airline's long-standing financial distress and the breakdown of federal rescue talks.

Specifically, on May 3, 2026, CBS News reported on the failed government rescue deal, wherein Secretary Duffy actively disputed the airline's claim that the shutdown was solely due to recent fuel spikes, stating: "Spirit was in dire straits long before the war with Iran." (See CBS News, 

"Spirit Airlines shutting down after failed effort at government rescue deal," May 3, 2026. https://www.cbsnews.com/news/spirit-airlines-shutting-down-failed-rescue-deal/).

Further, public reporting confirms that intensive, eleventh-hour negotiations took place between the administration and Spirit’s creditors regarding a bailout that "would have given the U.S. government a 90% stake in the airline." (Id.). The DOT cannot credibly claim that records of these highly publicized government activities do not exist or are exempt from disclosure under the deliberative process privilege when the agency itself has already introduced these discussions into the public domain.

 FEE WAIVER JUSTIFICATION

We request a complete waiver of all search, review, and duplication fees associated with processing this request. Under 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(4)(A)(iii) and 49 C.F.R. § 7.43(c), an agency must waive fees when "disclosure of the information is in the public interest because it is likely to contribute significantly to public understanding of the operations or activities of the government and is not primarily in the commercial interest of the requester." This request meets and exceeds every legal benchmark for a mandatory fee waiver:

1. The Subject Matter Concerns the Operations and Activities of the Federal Government. The requested records directly concern the DOT’s regulatory oversight, economic monitoring, and executive branch bailout negotiations involving a major interstate carrier.

2. Disclosure is Meaningfully Informative and Significantly Contributes to Public Understanding. Spirit’s collapse is not a routine corporate restructuring; it is a seismic event. As the first major bankruptcy and total operational liquidation in the airline industry in a long time, it resulted in the immediate stranding of thousands of passengers and the loss of roughly 7,500 jobs. The public has been left in the dark about how a major carrier was allowed to reach the point of total collapse under federal watch. Disclosure of these communications will provide a vital, unprecedented window into the DOT’s consumer protection failures and the parameters of the failed $500 million taxpayer bailout.
Courts have routinely held that when a request involves matters of high-profile federal intervention and significant public financial implications, the "significant contribution" test is easily satisfied. See Better Gov't Ass'n v. Dep't of State, 780 F.2d 86 (D.C. Cir. 1986); Judicial Watch, Inc. v. Rossotti, 326 F.3d 1309, 1314 (D.C. Cir. 2003) (holding that demonstrating an ability to publicize disclosed information is sufficient for a fee waiver).

3. The Request is Not in the Commercial Interest of the Requester. We have no commercial interest in these records; our sole interest is transparency and accountability.
Given the historic nature of this airline collapse, any denial of this fee waiver will be viewed as arbitrary and capricious, and will be immediately appealed and fiercely litigated.

IV. PRODUCTION EXPECTATIONS AND VAUGHN INDEX DEMAND
If any portion of this request is denied or heavily redacted, we demand a detailed Vaughn index justifying each withholding, including the specific statutory exemption claimed, a description of the withheld material, and the specific harm that would result from disclosure. See Vaughn v. Rosen, 484 F.2d 820 (D.C. Cir. 1973). The DOT is reminded of its statutory obligation under the FOIA Improvement Act of 2016 to release records unless the agency "reasonably foresees that disclosure would harm an interest protected by an exemption." 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(8)(A). Blanket assertions of Exemption 5 (Deliberative Process Privilege) for communications involving outside creditors and lobbyists will not survive legal scrutiny.
We expect a determination regarding this request within the statutory twenty (20) working-day limit. Please provide all responsive records in native electronic format (.PDF for documents, .PST or .MSG for emails).

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

SAM RICHARDSON
